news_summary: Breakthroughs in understanding rare vaccine side effects, dietary habits for weight control, early Alzheimer's detection, and genetic influences on sex development highlight today's advances in health research.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans la compréhension des effets secondaires rares des vaccins, des habitudes alimentaires pour le contrôle du poids, du dépistage précoce de la maladie d'Alzheimer et des influences génétiques sur le développement sexuel illustrent les progrès actuels de la recherche en santé.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la comprensión de los efectos secundarios poco frecuentes de las vacunas, los hábitos alimenticios para el control del peso, la detección precoz del Alzheimer y las influencias genéticas en el desarrollo sexual destacan los progresos actuales en la investigación sanitaria.

articles.title: Scientists finally crack mystery of rare COVID vaccine blood clots
articles.summary: Researchers have uncovered why a rare blood clotting disorder can occur after certain COVID-19 vaccines or adenovirus infections. The immune system can mistakenly target a normal blood protein (PF4) after confusing it with a viral protein. This triggers clotting in extremely rare cases. The breakthrough means vaccines can now be redesigned to avoid this reaction while staying effective.
articles.content:
Researchers led by Flinders University, working with international collaborators, have uncovered how a rare blood clotting condition can develop after certain COVID19 adenovirus-based vaccines or even after a natural adenovirus infection.

The team, which included scientists from Flinders University and Greifswald University, discovered that in a very small number of people, the immune system can mistakenly confuse a normal adenovirus protein with a human blood protein called platelet factor 4 (or PF4).

Immune System Mix-Up Triggers Clotting

When this confusion occurs, the body produces antibodies that activate clotting. Although this reaction is extremely rare, identifying the exact cause is a major step forward. It means vaccine developers can now modify the adenovirus protein to prevent this issue and improve vaccine safety.

Flinders University researcher Dr. Jing Jing Wang says the findings offer a clear path forward.

"By modifying or removing this specific adenovirus protein, future vaccines can avoid this extremely rare reaction while continuing to provide strong protection against disease," says Dr. Wang.

From VITT Discovery to Molecular Explanation

This study is part of a broader international effort to understand vaccine-induced immune thrombocytopenia and thrombosis (known as VITT), a condition first identified during the COVID19 pandemic in 2021. It appeared after the use of adenovirus vector-based vaccines, including the Oxford-AstraZeneca vaccine widely used in Australia.

Scientists determined that VITT is caused by a harmful autoantibody that targets PF4.

Earlier work led by Dr. Wang and Professor Tom Gordon, Head of Immunology at SA Pathology in South Australia, decoded the structure of this PF4 antibody in 2022. That study also identified a genetic risk factor linked to an antibody gene called IGLV3.21*02. The discovery connected cases across different countries and helped establish a long-term collaboration with Greifswald University, led by Professor Andreas Greinacher.

Evidence From Infection and Vaccination

In 2023, Professor Ted Warkentin from McMaster University in Canada reported a nearly identical condition caused by the same PF4 antibody in patients who had natural adenovirus (common cold) infections, with some cases proving fatal.

A follow-up study in 2024 involving Flinders, Greifswald, and McMaster Universities showed that antibodies from vaccine-related and infection-related cases were indistinguishable. This pointed to the adenovirus itself, rather than a specific vaccine ingredient, as the source of the problem. However, the exact molecular mechanism was still unclear at that time.

Breakthrough Study Reveals Molecular Trigger

According to Professor Tom Gordon, the latest findings, published in the New England Journal of Medicine, mark the culmination of years of global research.

"It has been a fascinating journey with an outstanding international team of collaborators to complete a trilogy of publications in the New England Journal of Medicine to solve the mystery of this new group of blood clotting disorders, and potentially translate our discoveries into safer vaccines," says Professor Gordon.

Dr. Wang explained that the team's detailed molecular analysis finally uncovered the missing piece.

"A novel aspect of the paper was our use of powerful mass spectrometry sequencing to identify molecular mimicry between the adenovirus vector protein and the PF4 culprit target," she says.

"This was the missing link that explains how a normal immune response can, in very rare cases, become harmful."

Safer Future Vaccines Within Reach

Immunologist Professor James McCluskey from the University of Melbourne and the Peter Doherty Institute called the work a major scientific milestone.

"It is a brilliant piece of molecular sleuthing, the culmination of a body of work that unravels the genetic and structural basis for how a normal immune response to a virus protein leads to pathogenic autoimmunity," says Professor McCluskey.

With the exact trigger now identified, researchers say vaccine developers can adjust the pVII protein in adenovirus-based vaccines to remove this rare risk.

These findings are expected to support the development of safer vaccines that remain effective and widely accessible, especially in regions where adenovirus-based vaccines play an important role in preventing disease.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough explaining the rare blood clotting disorder linked to certain COVID-19 adenovirus-based vaccines and natural adenovirus infections. This discovery has broad public health implications, enabling vaccine developers to redesign safer vaccines that maintain effectiveness, benefiting global populations, especially in regions reliant on these vaccines. The article provides detailed context about the research, international collaboration, and potential future impact, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover why rare blood clots happen after some COVID vaccines
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Some COVID-19 vaccines use a virus called adenovirus to help protect people. A very small number of people who got these vaccines developed a rare blood clotting problem. Scientists have been trying to understand why this happens.

**What happened** – Researchers from Flinders University and other international teams found that the immune system can confuse a normal blood protein called PF4 with a protein from the adenovirus. This confusion causes the body to produce antibodies that trigger blood clots. They used advanced techniques to find the exact molecular reason behind this reaction.

**Impact** – This discovery explains the cause of the rare blood clotting disorder linked to some COVID vaccines and even natural adenovirus infections. Knowing the exact trigger means vaccine makers can change the virus protein in the vaccines to stop this problem from happening, making vaccines safer while still protecting people well.

**What's next step** – Vaccine developers will work on modifying the adenovirus protein in their vaccines to remove the risk of this rare clotting reaction. This will help create safer vaccines that can be used widely, especially in places that rely on adenovirus-based vaccines for disease prevention.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found the cause of rare blood clots after some COVID vaccines, paving the way for safer vaccines in the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent pourquoi de rares caillots sanguins surviennent après certains vaccins COVID
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Certains vaccins contre la COVID-19 utilisent un virus appelé adénovirus pour protéger les personnes. Un très petit nombre de personnes vaccinées ont développé un problème rare de caillots sanguins. Les scientifiques cherchaient à comprendre pourquoi.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université Flinders et d'autres équipes internationales ont découvert que le système immunitaire peut confondre une protéine sanguine normale appelée PF4 avec une protéine de l'adénovirus. Cette confusion pousse le corps à produire des anticorps qui déclenchent la formation de caillots. Ils ont utilisé des techniques avancées pour trouver la cause moléculaire exacte de cette réaction.

**Impact** – Cette découverte explique la cause du trouble rare des caillots sanguins lié à certains vaccins COVID et même aux infections naturelles par l'adénovirus. Connaître ce déclencheur permet maintenant aux fabricants de vaccins de modifier la protéine virale pour éviter ce problème, rendant les vaccins plus sûrs tout en restant efficaces.

**Prochaine étape** – Les développeurs de vaccins vont travailler à modifier la protéine d'adénovirus dans leurs vaccins pour éliminer le risque de cette réaction rare. Cela aidera à créer des vaccins plus sûrs, surtout dans les régions qui dépendent de ces vaccins pour prévenir les maladies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont trouvé la cause des rares caillots sanguins après certains vaccins COVID, ouvrant la voie à des vaccins plus sûrs à l'avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren por qué ocurren coágulos sanguíneos raros tras algunas vacunas COVID
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algunas vacunas contra el COVID-19 usan un virus llamado adenovirus para proteger a las personas. Un número muy pequeño de personas que recibieron estas vacunas desarrollaron un problema raro de coágulos sanguíneos. Los científicos han estado tratando de entender por qué.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Flinders y otros equipos internacionales encontraron que el sistema inmunológico puede confundir una proteína normal de la sangre llamada PF4 con una proteína del adenovirus. Esta confusión hace que el cuerpo produzca anticuerpos que provocan coágulos. Usaron técnicas avanzadas para encontrar la razón molecular exacta detrás de esta reacción.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento explica la causa del raro problema de coágulos sanguíneos vinculado a algunas vacunas COVID e incluso a infecciones naturales por adenovirus. Saber el desencadenante exacto significa que los fabricantes de vacunas pueden cambiar la proteína del virus en las vacunas para evitar este problema, haciendo las vacunas más seguras y efectivas.

**Próximo paso** – Los desarrolladores de vacunas trabajarán en modificar la proteína del adenovirus en sus vacunas para eliminar el riesgo de esta rara reacción. Esto ayudará a crear vacunas más seguras que se puedan usar ampliamente, especialmente en lugares que dependen de vacunas basadas en adenovirus para prevenir enfermedades.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos encontraron la causa de los raros coágulos sanguíneos tras algunas vacunas COVID, abriendo el camino para vacunas más seguras en el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two intertwined molecular structures glowing gently: one shaped like a stylized adenovirus protein and the other like the human platelet factor 4 protein, connected by a delicate thread of light representing their mistaken identity, set against a soft, natural background of abstract laboratory glassware and scientific instruments symbolizing global collaboration and breakthrough discovery.

articles.title: Two simple eating habits linked to lower weight, study finds
articles.summary: A major study suggests that when you eat could play a key role in staying lean. People who fast longer overnight and start their day with an early breakfast were more likely to have a lower BMI years later. Scientists think this is because eating earlier aligns better with the body’s internal clock. But skipping breakfast as part of intermittent fasting didn’t offer the same advantage—and may even be tied to unhealthy habits.
articles.content:
Maintaining a healthy weight may depend not only on food choices but also on meal timing. A study published in the International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity found that two habits are linked to a lower body mass index (BMI) over time: extending the overnight fasting period and eating breakfast early. The research was led by the Barcelona Institute for Global Health (ISGlobal), a centre supported by the "la Caixa" Foundation.

The findings are based on data from more than 7,000 adults between the ages of 40-65 who are part of the GCAT | Genomes for Life cohort, led by the Germans Trias i Pujol Research Institute (IGTP). In 2018, participants provided details about their height, weight, meal timing, lifestyle habits, and socioeconomic background through questionnaires.

Five years later, in 2023, more than 3,000 of these individuals returned for follow-up assessments. Researchers recorded updated measurements and collected new survey data, allowing them to track changes and identify patterns over time.

Early Eating and Longer Fasting Linked to Lower BMI

"Our results, in line with other recent studies, suggest that extending the overnight fast could help maintain a healthy weight if accompanied by an early dinner and an early breakfast. We think this may be because eating earlier in the day is more in line with circadian rhythms and allows for better calorie burning and appetite regulation, which can help maintain a healthy weight. However, it is too soon to draw definitive conclusions, so recommendations will have to wait for more robust evidence," explains Luciana Pons-Muzzo, researcher at ISGlobal at the time of the study and currently at IESE Business School.

Gender Differences and Lifestyle Patterns

When researchers compared results by gender, they found notable differences. Women generally had lower BMI, followed the Mediterranean diet more closely, and were less likely to drink alcohol. At the same time, they reported poorer mental health and were more often responsible for household or family supervision.

Using a method called 'cluster analysis', the team grouped participants with similar characteristics. One small group of men stood out. These individuals typically ate their first meal after 14:00 and fasted for about 17 hours. Compared to others, they were more likely to smoke and drink alcohol, less physically active, and less likely to follow the Mediterranean diet. They also tended to have lower levels of education and higher rates of unemployment. Researchers did not observe a similar pattern among women.

Intermittent Fasting and Breakfast Skipping

"There are different ways of practising what is known as 'intermittent fasting' and our study relates to one of them, which is overnight fasting. What we observed in a subgroup of men who do intermittent fasting by skipping breakfast is that this practice has no effect on body weight. Other intervention studies in participants with obesity have shown that this tactic is no more effective than reducing calorie intake in reducing body weight in the long term," says Camille Lassale, ISGlobal researcher and senior co-author of the study.

Chrononutrition and the Body's Internal Clock

"Our research is part of an emerging field of research known as 'chrononutrition', which focuses not only on analysing what we eat, but also the times of day and the number of times we eat," says Anna Palomar-Cros, researcher at ISGlobal at the time of the study and currently at IDIAP Jordi Gol. "At the basis of this research is the knowledge that unusual food intake patterns can conflict with the circadian system, the set of internal clocks that regulate the cycles of night and day and the physiological processes that must accompany them," she adds.

Earlier Meals Linked to Broader Health Benefits

This study builds on earlier ISGlobal research in chrononutrition. Previous findings have shown that eating dinner and breakfast earlier in the day is associated with a lower risk of cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes, reinforcing the idea that meal timing plays a meaningful role in long-term health.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant scientific study showing that simple eating habits, specifically longer overnight fasting and early breakfast, are linked to lower BMI and potentially better health outcomes. The study is based on a large cohort and provides meaningful insights into chrononutrition, which can influence public health recommendations broadly. The findings have tangible implications for improving weight management and reducing risks of chronic diseases, making it inspiring and hopeful for many people.
articles.personality_title: Study finds early breakfast and longer overnight fast linked to lower weight
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A recent study looked at how the timing of meals might affect body weight. Instead of focusing only on what people eat, the researchers studied when people eat to see if it makes a difference to their health.

**What happened** – Scientists in Spain studied over 7,000 adults aged 40-65 who shared information about their eating habits, weight, and lifestyle in 2018. Five years later, more than 3,000 of these people were checked again. The study found that those who ate breakfast early and fasted longer overnight were more likely to have a lower body mass index (BMI), a measure of body fat. People who skipped breakfast as part of intermittent fasting did not show the same benefits and sometimes had less healthy habits.

**Impact** – This study is important because it shows that not just what we eat, but when we eat, affects our weight and health. Eating earlier fits better with the body’s natural internal clock, called the circadian rhythm, which helps the body use calories well and control appetite. The research also found that men who ate late and fasted by skipping breakfast often had riskier habits like smoking and drinking. This adds new understanding to how meal timing and lifestyle work together.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to do more research to confirm these findings and better understand how meal timing can help with weight control and disease prevention. This could lead to new advice about when to eat, not just what to eat, to stay healthy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Eating breakfast early and fasting longer overnight are linked to lower body weight and may support healthier living by aligning with the body’s natural clock.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude révèle que prendre un petit-déjeuner tôt et jeûner plus longtemps la nuit aide à maintenir un poids plus bas
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Une étude récente s’est intéressée au moment où les gens mangent pour voir si cela influence leur poids, plutôt que de se concentrer uniquement sur ce qu’ils mangent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques en Espagne ont étudié plus de 7 000 adultes âgés de 40 à 65 ans qui ont donné des informations sur leurs habitudes alimentaires, leur poids et leur mode de vie en 2018. Cinq ans plus tard, plus de 3 000 d’entre eux ont été réévalués. L’étude a montré que ceux qui prenaient leur petit-déjeuner tôt et jeûnaient plus longtemps la nuit avaient plus de chances d’avoir un indice de masse corporelle (IMC) plus bas. Ceux qui sautaient le petit-déjeuner dans le cadre du jeûne intermittent n’avaient pas les mêmes bénéfices et avaient parfois des habitudes moins saines.

**Impact** – Cette étude est importante car elle montre que le moment des repas influence le poids et la santé. Manger plus tôt correspond mieux à l’horloge interne du corps, appelée rythme circadien, ce qui aide à mieux utiliser les calories et à contrôler l’appétit. Les chercheurs ont aussi observé que les hommes qui mangeaient tard et jeûnaient en sautant le petit-déjeuner avaient souvent des comportements plus risqués comme fumer et boire de l’alcool. Cela apporte une nouvelle compréhension du lien entre le moment des repas et le mode de vie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent approfondir leurs recherches pour confirmer ces résultats et mieux comprendre comment le moment des repas peut aider à contrôler le poids et prévenir les maladies. Cela pourrait mener à de nouveaux conseils sur quand manger, pas seulement quoi manger, pour rester en bonne santé.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Prendre un petit-déjeuner tôt et jeûner plus longtemps la nuit est lié à un poids corporel plus bas et peut favoriser une meilleure santé en respectant l’horloge naturelle du corps.
articles.personality_title_es: Un estudio encuentra que desayunar temprano y ayunar más horas durante la noche se asocia con menor peso
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Un estudio reciente analizó cómo el horario de las comidas puede afectar el peso corporal, enfocándose no solo en qué se come, sino también en cuándo se come.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos en España estudiaron a más de 7,000 adultos de 40 a 65 años que compartieron información sobre sus hábitos alimenticios, peso y estilo de vida en 2018. Cinco años después, más de 3,000 de ellos fueron evaluados nuevamente. El estudio encontró que quienes desayunaban temprano y ayunaban más tiempo durante la noche tenían más probabilidades de tener un índice de masa corporal (IMC) más bajo. Quienes se saltaban el desayuno como parte del ayuno intermitente no mostraron los mismos beneficios y a veces tenían hábitos menos saludables.

**Impacto** – Este estudio es importante porque muestra que el momento de las comidas influye en el peso y la salud. Comer temprano está más alineado con el reloj interno del cuerpo, llamado ritmo circadiano, que ayuda a usar mejor las calorías y controlar el apetito. También se observó que los hombres que comían tarde y ayunaban saltándose el desayuno tenían hábitos de riesgo como fumar y beber alcohol. Esto aporta una nueva visión sobre cómo el horario de las comidas y el estilo de vida están conectados.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren hacer más investigaciones para confirmar estos hallazgos y entender mejor cómo el horario de las comidas puede ayudar a controlar el peso y prevenir enfermedades. Esto podría llevar a nuevas recomendaciones sobre cuándo comer, no solo qué comer, para mantener la salud.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Desayunar temprano y ayunar más tiempo durante la noche está ligado a un menor peso corporal y puede ayudar a vivir más saludablemente al respetar el reloj natural del cuerpo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a serene early morning kitchen scene bathed in soft natural light, featuring a simple table set with an early breakfast of wholesome Mediterranean foods like fruit, whole grains, and olive oil, alongside a subtle clock showing dawn hours; nearby, a gently glowing hourglass symbolizes an extended overnight fasting period, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones with no human figures.

articles.title: Your nose could detect Alzheimer’s years before symptoms begin
articles.summary: Losing your sense of smell might signal Alzheimer’s far earlier than expected. Scientists found that immune cells in the brain actively destroy smell-related nerve fibers after detecting abnormal signals on their surfaces. This damage begins in early stages of the disease, well before cognitive decline. The discovery could help identify at-risk patients sooner and improve treatment timing.
articles.content:
A declining sense of smell may be one of the earliest warning signs of Alzheimer's disease, appearing even before noticeable memory problems. New research from scientists at DZNE and Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität München (LMU) offers fresh insight into why this happens. The study points to the brain's immune system as a key player, showing that it may mistakenly attack nerve fibers that are essential for detecting odors. Published in Nature Communications, the research combines evidence from mice and humans, including brain tissue analysis and so-called PET scanning. These findings could help improve early detection and open the door to earlier treatment.

According to the researchers, smell-related problems arise when immune cells in the brain, known as "microglia," begin removing connections between two important regions: the olfactory bulb and the locus coeruleus. The olfactory bulb, located in the forebrain, processes signals from scent receptors in the nose. The locus coeruleus, found in the brainstem, helps regulate this process through long nerve fibers that extend to the olfactory bulb.

"The locus coeruleus regulates a variety physiological mechanisms. These include, for example, cerebral blood flow, sleep-wake cycles, and sensory processing. The latter applies, in particular, also to the sense of smell," says Dr. Lars Paeger, a scientist at DZNE and LMU. "Our study suggests that in early Alzheimer's disease, changes occur in the nerve fibers linking the locus coeruleus to the olfactory bulb. These alterations signal to the microglia that affected fibers are defective or superfluous. Consequently, the microglia break them down."

Alterations in the membrane

The team, led by Dr. Lars Paeger and co-author Prof. Dr. Jochen Herms, identified specific changes in the membranes of these nerve fibers. They found that phosphatidylserine, a fatty molecule normally located on the inside of a neuron's membrane, had shifted to the outer surface.

"Presence of phosphatidylserine at the outer site of the cell membrane is known to be an "eat-me" signal for microglia. In the olfactory bulb, this is usually associated with a process called synaptic pruning, which serves to remove unnecessary or dysfunctional neuronal connections," explains Paeger. "In our situation, we assume that the shift in membrane composition is triggered by hyperactivity of the affected neurons due to Alzheimer's disease. That is, these neurons exhibit abnormal firing."

Evidence From Animal Models, Human Tissue, and Brain Scans

The conclusions are supported by multiple lines of evidence. The researchers studied mice that show Alzheimer's-like features, examined brain tissue from deceased patients, and analyzed positron emission tomography (PET) scans from individuals with Alzheimer's or mild cognitive impairment.

"Smell issues in Alzheimer's disease and damage to the associated nerves have been discussed for some time. However, the causes were unclear until yet. Now, our ﬁndings point to an immunological mechanism as cause for such dysfunctions -- and, in particular, that such events already arise in the early stages of Alzheimer's disease," says Joachim Herms, a research group leader at DZNE and LMU as well as a member of the Munich-based "SyNergy" Cluster of Excellence.

Implications for Early Diagnosis and Treatment

So-called amyloid-beta antibodies have recently become available for the treatment of Alzheimer's. For these therapies to work effectively, they must be given early in the disease process. This is where the new findings could make a difference.

"Our findings could pave the way for the early identification of patients at risk of developing Alzheimer's, enabling them to undergo comprehensive testing to confirm the diagnosis before cognitive problems arise. This would allow earlier intervention with amyloid-beta antibodies, increasing the probability of a positive response," says Herms.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that links early loss of smell to Alzheimer's disease through immune system activity in the brain. This finding has broad implications for early diagnosis and timely treatment of Alzheimer's, potentially benefiting many people worldwide. The research is well-supported by evidence from animal models, human tissue, and brain scans, providing substantial context and detail about the breakthrough.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find loss of smell could signal Alzheimer’s years early
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Alzheimer's disease is a condition that affects the brain, causing memory loss and other problems. Researchers have long searched for ways to detect it early, before symptoms become obvious.

**What happened** – Scientists from DZNE and Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität München discovered that losing the sense of smell may be one of the earliest signs of Alzheimer's. They found that immune cells in the brain, called microglia, attack nerve fibers that connect the nose to the brain when they detect abnormal signals. This damage starts early, even before memory issues begin. The study involved experiments on mice, analysis of human brain tissue, and brain scans.

**Impact** – This discovery explains why people with early Alzheimer's often lose their sense of smell. It shows that the brain’s immune system mistakenly damages important nerve connections. Understanding this process could help doctors identify people at risk much earlier than before. Early detection is important because new treatments work best when given early.

**What’s next step** – Researchers hope this finding will lead to tests that check for smell loss as a sign of Alzheimer’s risk. Patients identified this way could get further examinations and, if needed, start treatments like amyloid-beta antibodies sooner. Early treatment may slow the disease and improve quality of life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Losing the sense of smell may be an early warning sign of Alzheimer’s caused by immune cells damaging brain nerves, offering a chance for earlier diagnosis and treatment.
articles.personality_title_fr: La perte d’odorat pourrait signaler la maladie d’Alzheimer des années à l’avance
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie d’Alzheimer affecte le cerveau et provoque des pertes de mémoire. Les chercheurs cherchent des moyens de la détecter tôt, avant l’apparition des symptômes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques du DZNE et de l’Université Ludwig-Maximilians de Munich ont découvert que la perte de l’odorat peut être un des premiers signes d’Alzheimer. Ils ont trouvé que les cellules immunitaires du cerveau, appelées microglies, attaquent les fibres nerveuses reliant le nez au cerveau lorsqu’elles détectent des signaux anormaux. Ces dégâts commencent tôt, avant les troubles de mémoire. L’étude a utilisé des souris, des tissus humains et des scans cérébraux.

**Impact** – Cette découverte explique pourquoi les malades d’Alzheimer perdent souvent l’odorat au début. Elle montre que le système immunitaire du cerveau détruit par erreur des connexions nerveuses importantes. Comprendre cela peut aider à détecter la maladie beaucoup plus tôt. La détection précoce est importante car les nouveaux traitements sont plus efficaces au début.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent développer des tests basés sur la perte d’odorat pour identifier les personnes à risque. Ces patients pourraient passer des examens complémentaires et commencer plus tôt des traitements comme les anticorps anti-amyloïdes. Un traitement précoce peut ralentir la maladie et améliorer la vie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La perte de l’odorat pourrait être un signe précoce d’Alzheimer causé par des cellules immunitaires qui endommagent les nerfs du cerveau, ouvrant la voie à un diagnostic et un traitement plus précoces.
articles.personality_title_es: La pérdida del olfato podría indicar Alzheimer años antes de los síntomas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de Alzheimer afecta al cerebro y causa pérdida de memoria. Los científicos buscan formas de detectarla temprano, antes de que aparezcan los síntomas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del DZNE y la Universidad Ludwig-Maximilians de Múnich descubrieron que la pérdida del olfato puede ser una de las primeras señales de Alzheimer. Encontraron que las células inmunitarias del cerebro, llamadas microglías, atacan las fibras nerviosas que conectan la nariz con el cerebro cuando detectan señales anormales. Este daño comienza temprano, antes de los problemas de memoria. El estudio usó ratones, tejidos humanos y escáneres cerebrales.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo explica por qué las personas con Alzheimer temprano suelen perder el olfato. Muestra que el sistema inmunitario del cerebro daña por error conexiones nerviosas importantes. Entender esto puede ayudar a detectar la enfermedad mucho antes. Detectarla temprano es clave porque los tratamientos nuevos funcionan mejor al principio.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan que este descubrimiento lleve a pruebas que usen la pérdida del olfato para identificar a personas en riesgo. Estas personas podrían hacerse más exámenes y comenzar tratamientos como anticuerpos contra la amiloide-beta antes. El tratamiento temprano puede frenar la enfermedad y mejorar la calidad de vida.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La pérdida del olfato puede ser una señal temprana de Alzheimer causada por células inmunitarias que dañan nervios cerebrales, ofreciendo una oportunidad para un diagnóstico y tratamiento más pronto.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an abstract brain section highlighting a glowing olfactory bulb connected by delicate, golden nerve fibers to a softly illuminated locus coeruleus, with gentle, translucent microglia-like figures carefully pruning the fibers, set against a calm background of natural, muted earth tones and soft light.

articles.title: Female mice grow testes after this single DNA tweak
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 09 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01120-8Small changes in the non-coding part of the genome have a key role in sex determination.
articles.content:
Small changes to the non-coding genome can alter whether a mouse embryo (pictured here at 9.5 days old) develops male or female sex organs.Credit: Sinclair Stammers/SPL

Female mouse embryos with a single letter change in a specific region of their DNA develop male reproductive organs, finds a study1 published today in Nature Communications.

Typically, female mouse embryos with two X chromosomes develop ovaries because a gene called Sox9 is suppressed. In male mouse embryos with XY chromosomes, the expression of Sox9 triggers testis development.

In male mice, Sox9 is controlled by a segment of non-coding DNA — part of the genome that does not encode proteins — called enhancer 13 (Enh13). Studies have shown that deleting Enh13 causes mice with XY chromosomes to develop female organs2, but until now, modifying Enh13 was not known to have any effects in female (XX) mice.

Nitzan Gonen, who studies sex determination at Bar-Ilan University in Ramat Gan, Israel, and her colleagues show that modifying both copies of Enh13 in female mice causes them to develop male genitals and small testes. Female mice carrying just one modified copy of Enh13 still developed female organs, however. The team suggests that Enh13 acts as both an enhancer and a silencer of Sox9, and is a site where the ‘battle of the sexes’ plays out.

This study is the first to unravel the mechanism that determines whether an embryo develops ovaries or testes, says Katie Ayers, a genetics researcher at the Murdoch Children’s Research Institute in Melbourne, Australia.

The region of DNA that the team modified is also important for human sex determination, says Ayers. Around 50% of people with disorders affecting their sexual development do not have a genetic diagnosis3. In part, that’s because sequencing to identify disease-causing mutations looks only at parts of the genome that encode proteins. Ayers says that researchers are increasingly studying non-coding areas of the genome, and that looking for small changes in the Enh13 region could identify other genetic alterations responsible for disorders of sex development.

Sex reversal goes both ways

Gonen was part of the team that discovered that Enh13 had a role in sex development. In 20182, the researchers showed that deleting this enhancer caused male mice with XY chromosomes to develop female organs. Then, in 2024, the team made a smaller modification — deleting parts of Enh13 that bind to transcription factors (proteins that regulate gene activity) — and found that it triggered the same sex reversal in XY mice3.
articles.reasoning: The study reveals a fundamental genetic mechanism controlling sex determination, with significant implications for understanding and diagnosing human disorders of sexual development. It advances scientific knowledge with potential broad medical benefits, especially for people with unexplained sex development disorders.
articles.personality_title: Small DNA change causes female mice to grow testes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Normally, female mice have two X chromosomes and develop ovaries, while male mice have XY chromosomes and develop testes. A gene called Sox9 controls this difference, and a part of the DNA called Enhancer 13 (Enh13) helps turn Sox9 on or off.

**What happened** – Scientists changed a single letter in the Enh13 DNA region in female mouse embryos. This small change made the female embryos grow male reproductive organs and small testes instead of ovaries. If only one copy of the DNA was changed, the mice stayed female. This discovery shows that Enh13 plays a key role in deciding whether an embryo becomes male or female.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it explains how a tiny change in a non-coding part of the genome (DNA that doesn’t make proteins) can control sex development. It also helps scientists understand why some people have differences in their sexual development that doctors cannot currently explain. This research points to new ways to study and diagnose these conditions by looking at the non-coding DNA.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will likely study the Enh13 region more closely in humans to find out if similar DNA changes cause sex development disorders. This could lead to better genetic tests and treatments for people with unexplained differences in sex development.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Changing one letter in a small DNA region can switch female mice to develop male organs, revealing a hidden genetic switch for sex development.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un petit changement dans l’ADN fait pousser des testicules chez des souris femelles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Normalement, les souris femelles ont deux chromosomes X et développent des ovaires, tandis que les mâles ont XY et développent des testicules. Un gène appelé Sox9 contrôle cette différence, et une partie de l’ADN appelée l’amplificateur 13 (Enh13) aide à activer ou désactiver Sox9.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont modifié une seule lettre dans la région Enh13 chez des embryons de souris femelles. Ce petit changement a fait que ces embryons femelles développent des organes reproducteurs mâles et de petits testicules au lieu d’ovaires. Si une seule copie de l’ADN était modifiée, les souris restaient femelles. Cette découverte montre qu’Enh13 joue un rôle clé dans la décision du sexe de l’embryon.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle explique comment un tout petit changement dans une partie non codante de l’ADN (qui ne fabrique pas de protéines) peut contrôler le développement du sexe. Elle aide aussi à comprendre pourquoi certaines personnes ont des différences sexuelles que les médecins ne peuvent pas encore expliquer. Cette recherche ouvre la voie à de nouvelles méthodes pour étudier et diagnostiquer ces conditions en regardant l’ADN non codant.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement étudier de plus près la région Enh13 chez l’humain pour voir si des changements similaires causent des troubles du développement sexuel. Cela pourrait améliorer les tests génétiques et les traitements pour les personnes avec des différences sexuelles inexpliquées.

**Une phrase clé** – Modifier une seule lettre dans une petite région d’ADN peut faire que des souris femelles développent des organes mâles, révélant un interrupteur génétique caché du développement sexuel.
articles.personality_title_es: Un pequeño cambio en el ADN hace que ratones hembra desarrollen testículos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Normalmente, las ratones hembra tienen dos cromosomas X y desarrollan ovarios, mientras que los machos tienen XY y desarrollan testículos. Un gen llamado Sox9 controla esta diferencia, y una parte del ADN llamada Potenciador 13 (Enh13) ayuda a activar o desactivar Sox9.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos cambiaron una sola letra en la región Enh13 del ADN en embriones de ratones hembra. Este pequeño cambio hizo que los embriones hembras desarrollaran órganos reproductores masculinos y testículos pequeños en lugar de ovarios. Si solo una copia del ADN estaba cambiada, las ratones seguían siendo hembras. Este hallazgo muestra que Enh13 juega un papel clave en decidir si un embrión será macho o hembra.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque explica cómo un cambio muy pequeño en una parte del ADN que no codifica proteínas puede controlar el desarrollo sexual. También ayuda a entender por qué algunas personas tienen diferencias en su desarrollo sexual que los médicos no pueden explicar. Esta investigación abre nuevas formas para estudiar y diagnosticar estas condiciones mirando el ADN no codificante.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente estudiarán más a fondo la región Enh13 en humanos para ver si cambios similares causan trastornos del desarrollo sexual. Esto podría mejorar las pruebas genéticas y tratamientos para personas con diferencias sexuales sin explicación.

**Una frase clave** – Cambiar una sola letra en una pequeña región del ADN puede hacer que ratones hembra desarrollen órganos masculinos, revelando un interruptor genético oculto para el desarrollo sexual.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two intertwined mouse embryos in soft natural tones, one subtly glowing with delicate, stylized DNA strands forming a glowing single-letter change in a specific non-coding genome region, surrounded by abstract representations of developing reproductive organs shifting from ovaries to small testes, symbolizing the genetic toggle of sex determination.
